rows: Your family were master craftsmen, stretching back centuries to the days when the Sforzas ruled in Milano. For the most part they all worked in wood, but in the 19th century the mechanization of the Industrial Revolution nearly put your family business under. It was your grandfather who hit on the idea of the hand crafted weapon, a past time he had dabbled in since boyhood, and now had turned into a full time career. The Rizzini name soon became synonmous with the sort of elegant, high end pieces of art that the wealthy and titled all across Europe desired. The art was passed from your grandfather, to your father, and then to you. While they were all good, you were perhaps the best of the three. You were trained from your grandfather's knee, and studied with the best artists in the field in Europe. You had all of the potential to make the Rizzini name truly historic. Your fortune seemed set. You married the lovely Alicia when barely twenty-one, and you had your son, Vittorio before your first anniversary. Your life could not be more ideal. Tragedy struck, however, in the form of war and epidemic. You were called up to serve in the war, safely away from the front as you oversaw instead the creating of arms for the soldiers, a task which nearly stiffled your creativity, if it weren't for the fact that you at least were safe from the bombs and the tanks. However, not everyone was so lucky. The Spanish Flu outbreak that swept the world took your wife and young son. You weren't even there to be with them. Crushed and broken, you were given leave to return home, but without the will to pick up the pieces. Years flew by in a drunken haze of alochol and opium, until word finally reached you that your father was dying. Realizing there was no one left to take the business, you returned to your first love. It was hard, going back to your art, but the pain and the brokenness of the last few years gave way to some of your best work, pieces that were more art than weapon, even some works that weren't weapons at all, but sculptures. The name of Rizzini began to gain back its reputation when Constanza Clementi found you. She'd heard of your work, of course, and approached you requesting a particular kind of weapon. You could do it, of course, it was a straight forward affair of a personalized weapon, but she liked it well enough to offer you the gift of the embrace. She could help you ensure your work would live not just for your lifetime, but many to come, if you agreed. Despite some of your other inclinations, you agreed.  Milan was not safe for the likes of a Toreador neonate, and your sire spirited you first to London, then America, where you lived in Chicago for some time, under the guise of creating the American branch of your company. As the years went by you have left your company in the hands of retainers who have kept up the name of Rizzini, pretending that it is the son and now the grandson who are the keepers of the family tradition. But Chicago has had its own host of problems, and your own restlessness has set in. You are seeking new vistas for your artistic expression. Los Angeles is a domain returning to Camarilla control, and it could be the sort of new vista where you could refresh your artistic vision. And given the sort of clientele in California, (you already have an extensive list from there), you perhaps could do good work out there.
Your relationship to these people is described. This may mean they do not know you.
Diversify. Sculpture is good, but weapons got you into this world. Have some fun with your art.
Stock the court. Find people to sell your weapons to, or find a way to make your weapons useful to the court members who need them most
